text: Drawing inspiration from the recent synthesis of SrB3C3 and LaB3C3 sodalite-type clathrates, we employ density functional theory (DFT) to investigate the stability and superconducting properties of the XB3C3 family, including binary-guest XYB6C6 variants. Using DFT-chemical pressure analysis on members containing alkaline earth metals (X = Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba), we demonstrate that the size of the guest cation is a critical factor for driving ambient-pressure dynamical stability by optimizing the fit within the borocarbide cages. High-throughput screening of 105 binary-guest clathrates (XYB6C6) identifies 22 systems that are dynamically stable at 1 atmosphere, including 19 previously unreported phases. Our results show that the superconducting transition temperature (Tc) is highly sensitive to the average oxidation state of the guest metals; shifting the Fermi level to coincide with peaks in the density of states allows for significant tuning of Tc. Notably, KPbB6C6 is predicted to be a strong-coupling superconductor with an ambient-pressure Tc of 88 K, surpassing the boiling point of liquid nitrogen. Furthermore, we find that configurational and vibrational entropy facilitate the stabilization of these phases under high-pressure, high-temperature conditions, suggesting they can be synthesized and subsequently quenched to ambient pressure. These findings establish metallic, covalently bonded borocarbide clathrates as a robust platform for the design of conventional high-temperature superconductors.
